raw_case:
Atypical Imaging Findings in a Cortisol-producing Adrenal Adenoma Predominantly Composed of Lipid-poor Compact Cells


Abstract

Cortisol-producing adrenal adenomas consist of a mixture of lipid-rich clear and lipid-poor compact cells in varying proportions. Most adenomas are mainly composed of lipid-rich clear cells and typically exhibit low computed tomography (CT) attenuation values, high uptake on 131I-adosterol scintigraphy, and mild accumulation on 18F-fluorodeoxyglucose positron emission tomography/CT. However, adenomas predominantly composed of lipid-poor compact cells are rare, with limited evidence regarding their imaging characteristics. A 27-year-old woman with weight gain and a moon face was referred to our hospital. She presented with hypertension, dyslipidemia, low plasma ACTH levels, and autonomous cortisol secretion. We diagnosed this patient with ACTH-independent Cushing syndrome with a left adrenal tumor. The adrenal tumor exhibited imaging findings atypical for an adenoma, including high CT attenuation values, negative uptake on 131I-adosterol scintigraphy, and strong accumulation on 18F-fluorodeoxyglucose positron emission tomography/CT. Histopathological analysis indicated that the tumor was a rare type of adenoma, predominantly composed of lipid-poor compact cells. These findings and recent reports suggest that the proportion of compact cells can influence the imaging findings. Therefore, in the differential diagnosis, it is important to recognize that cortisol-producing adrenal adenomas predominantly composed of compact cells can present with atypical imaging findings.

Introduction

ACTH-independent Cushing syndrome arises from excessive cortisol production by adrenal tumors, primarily benign adenomas [1]. Cortisol-producing adrenal adenomas consist of a mixture of lipid-rich clear and lipid-poor compact cells in varying proportions, with the lipid content depending on the ratio of these 2 cell types. Most adenomas are mainly composed of clear cells with abundant intracellular lipids, resulting in low unenhanced computed tomography (CT) attenuation values (typically <10 Hounsfield units) because of their high lipid content [2, 3]. 131I-adosterol scintigraphy is commonly used in Japan, showing high uptake in cortisol-producing adenomas [4]. Adrenocortical carcinomas can also overproduce cortisol, displaying high unenhanced CT attenuation values, and sometimes negative uptake on 131I-adosterol scintigraphy [5, 6]. To distinguish between benign adenomas and carcinomas, 18F-fluorodeoxyglucose positron emission tomography/CT (18F-FDG PET/CT) is reported to be a useful tool [7]. Unlike typical clear cell-rich adenomas, 20% to 30% of adenomas have a higher proportion of compact cells with poor lipid content, resulting in a lipid-poor appearance. However, adenomas predominantly composed of compact cells are rare [3], and there is limited evidence regarding their imaging characteristics, posing challenges for diagnosis. Here, we present a case of a young woman with ACTH-independent Cushing syndrome resulting from a compact cell-dominant adenoma who exhibited atypical imaging findings. The relationship between the postoperative histopathological analyses and imaging results is also discussed.

Case Presentation

A 27-year-old woman with a left adrenal tumor was admitted to our hospital. She had no medical history until the age of 24 years, when she was diagnosed with hypertension and dyslipidemia during a medical checkup and experienced weight gain. At age 25 years, she began treatment with azilsartan (an angiotensin II receptor blocker) and esaxerenone (a selective mineralocorticoid receptor blocker) for hypertension at a local clinic. An abdominal CT scan conducted to investigate secondary hypertension revealed a left adrenal tumor, prompting her to undergo further examination at another hospital. Subsequently, Cushing syndrome was suspected, and she was referred to our hospital for further evaluation and treatment.

Diagnostic Assessment

On admission, her height was 168 cm, she weighed 71 kg (her maximum weight), and her body mass index was 25.1 kg/m2. Despite treatment for hypertension, her blood pressure had increased to 144/90 mmHg. She exhibited characteristic cushingoid features such as a moon face. Laboratory test findings were as follows: eosinopenia (42/μL, ref: 0-490/μL) and elevated total cholesterol (296 mg/dL [7.7 mmol/L], ref: 146-219 mg/dL [3.8-5.7 mmol/L]), with no electrolyte or glucose tolerance abnormalities observed. Her plasma ACTH level was low at 2.5 pg/mL (0.6 pmol/L, ref: 7-63 pg/mL [1.5-13.9 pmol/L]), whereas her serum cortisol level was within the reference range (15.2 μg/dL [419.4 nmol/L], ref: 5.9-17.0 μg/dL [162.8-469.0 nmol/L]). Her serum dehydroepiandrosterone-sulfate (DHEA-S) level was slightly decreased (159 ng/mL [5.5 μmol/L], ref: 180-3910 ng/mL [6.2-135.6 μmol/L]), 24-hour urinary free cortisol was markedly elevated at 302 μg/day (833.2 nmol/day, ref: 11.2-80.3 μg/day [30.9-221.6 nmol/day]), and 24-hour urinary 17-ketosteroid level was normal (7.87 mg/day [ref: 0.51-9.68 mg/day]). Her serum cortisol level during night-time sleep was elevated (16.9 μg/dL [466.3 nmol/L]). An incomplete suppression of serum cortisol level with a 1-mg overnight dexamethasone suppression test (14.4 μg/dL [397.3 nmol/L]) indicated autonomous cortisol secretion. Based on these results, a diagnosis of ACTH-independent Cushing syndrome was made. Plasma renin activity was not low (1.4 µg/L/h [ref: 0.2-2.3 µg/L/h]) and the plasma aldosterone concentration was not high (41.4 pg/mL [1.48 pmol/L], ref: 3.0-82.1 pg/mL [0.11-2.96 pmol/L]), indicating no autonomous aldosterone co-secretion.An abdominal CT scan revealed a left adrenal tumor approximately 2 cm in diameter with relatively high density (mean Hounsfield units, 44) (Fig. 1A) and heterogeneous contrast enhancement (Fig. 1B). 131I-adosterol scintigraphy showed no enhanced isotope accumulation in the tumor (Fig. 1C), whereas 18F-FDG PET/CT showed strong accumulation, with a maximum standardized uptake value (SUVmax) of 14.9 in the tumor (Fig. 1D). These radiological findings are not typical of cortisol-producing adenomas, and we could not rule out the possibility of adrenal carcinoma. Adrenal venous sampling (AVS) was performed to confirm cortisol overproduction in the tumor. Cortisol levels were measured in the adrenal veins (AV) and inferior vena cava (IVC). The cortisol gradient from the AV to the IVC was 0.95 on the right side and 6.55 on the left side. The left AV to right AV gradient of cortisol was 6.89 (serum cortisol level in right AV, 13.2 μg/dL [364.2 nmol/L]; left AV, 91.0 μg/dL [2510.7 nmol/L]; IVC, 13.9 μg/dL [383.5 nmol/L]). As previously reported [8], these results indicated that the left adrenal tumor was the source of excessive cortisol production.

Treatment

A laparoscopic left adrenalectomy was performed, and the tumor was completely resected.

Outcome and Follow-up

Macroscopic evaluation revealed a clearly demarcated, dark brown mass measuring 2 cm in diameter (Fig. 2A). Microscopic evaluation revealed that the tumor was mainly composed of compact cells with abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm and poor lipid content, including scattered lipofuscin granules (Figs. 2B-D). The Ki-67 index was 4% (Fig. 2E), which was compatible with a cortisol-producing adrenocortical adenoma. The lesion was diagnosed as an adrenocortical adenoma according to the Weiss score (1 of 9 points) [9]. Immunohistochemical analysis showed that the tumor cells were positive for P450scc, P450c17, P450c21, and CYP11B1; faintly positive for DHEA sulfotransferase (DHEA-ST); and negative for CYP11B2 (Figs. 3A-G); confirming the cortisol-producing ability of the tumor. The adjacent non-neoplastic adrenal cortex displayed cortical atrophy and was negative for DHEA-ST (Fig. 3H and I), reflecting long-term suppression of the hypothalamus-pituitary-adrenal axis.Hydrocortisone replacement therapy was initiated postoperatively and gradually tapered. Her blood pressure levels improved postoperatively, leading to antihypertensive drug discontinuation.

Discussion

Here, we present the case of a young woman with ACTH-independent Cushing syndrome. Her left adrenal tumor exhibited imaging findings atypical for an adenoma, including high unenhanced CT attenuation values, negative uptake on 131I-adosterol scintigraphy, and strong accumulation on 18F-FDG PET/CT, complicating the preoperative diagnosis. Histopathological analyses indicated a rare type of cortisol-producing adrenocortical adenoma predominantly composed of compact cells. These atypical imaging findings are likely associated with a compact cell-dominant histology.The first characteristic finding was high unenhanced CT attenuation values. Adrenal adenomas comprise a mixture of lipid-rich clear cells and lipid-poor compact cells in various proportions, and the clear cell/compact cell ratio is inversely correlated with the unenhanced CT attenuation values [10]. Typically, cortisol-producing adenomas show low unenhanced CT attenuation values because of a high proportion of lipid-rich clear cells. However, in our patient, the high proportion of lipid-poor compact cells contributed to the high unenhanced CT attenuation values.The second notable finding was the negative scintigraphy findings. 131I-adosterol scintigraphy has a very high sensitivity and specificity for ACTH-independent Cushing syndrome [11, 12] and is a useful tool for functional evaluation. Radioisotope accumulation appears to depend on the intracellular cholesterol ester pool. Radiopharmaceuticals used in 131I-adosterol scintigraphy, 131I-6β-iodomethylnorcholesterol, are taken up by adrenocortical cells through low-density lipoprotein receptors and accumulated as cholesterol esters after esterification [13]. Hormone-sensitive lipase (HSL), which catalyzes the conversion of free cholesterol from cholesterol esters, and acyl-CoA: cholesterol acyltransferase 1/2 (ACAT1/2), which catalyzes the conversion of free cholesterol into cholesterol esters, play important roles in maintaining intracellular cholesterol levels [14]. A recent study demonstrated that both HSL and ACAT1/2 activities are low in clear cells, maintaining the intracellular cholesterol ester pool [15]. In contrast, increased HSL activity compared to that of ACAT1/2 in compact cells promotes the conversion of cholesterol esters to free cholesterol, resulting in a shrinking of the intracellular cholesterol ester pool [15]. These findings suggest that the predominance of compact cells in the patient's adenoma resulted in less intracellular accumulation of esterified isotopes, contributing to the negative scintigraphy results.The third finding was high FDG accumulation. The average SUVmax of cortisol-producing adrenal adenomas in 18F-FDG PET/CT is 5.9 ± 3.0, which is higher than that of aldosterone-producing adenomas and nonfunctioning adenomas [16]. However, our patient had a higher SUVmax of 14.9. Cortisol-producing adenomas with a higher SUVmax have been reported to have more compact cells and higher expression of the glucose transporter (GLUT)-1,3, and genes related to glycolysis and aerobic metabolism. In addition, SUVmax and expression of these genes have shown strong positive correlations [17]. Therefore, we consider that the increased FDG uptake via GLUT-1,3 in compact cells led to the high SUVmax in our patient's adenoma.Similar atypical imaging findings have also been reported in adrenocortical carcinoma and black adenoma [6, 18]. In our patient, 18F-FDG PET/CT showed strong accumulation in the left adrenal tumor; however, adrenocortical carcinoma was excluded based on pathological findings. Black adenomas are characterized by abundant compact cells and fewer clear cells with pigmented granules containing lipofuscin in their cytoplasm [19]. Excessive lipofuscin deposition results in a black appearance [19]. Although our patient's adenoma had the same imaging findings as in black adenoma [17], the adenoma did not show a black appearance because of scattered lipofuscin granules. However, it had characteristics similar to those of black adenomas with respect to compact cell predominance. Therefore, we consider that a high proportion of compact cells, regardless of lipofuscin content, can lead to atypical imaging findings, as observed in our patient.Serum DHEA-S level was marginally decreased but not completely suppressed, despite strong suppression of ACTH. This may also be related to the compact cell predominance. Compact cells are hormonally active and have relatively higher expression of DHEA-ST than clear cells [20]. In our patient, the adjacent non-neoplastic adrenal cortex was immunonegative for DHEA-ST, but the tumor cells were faintly immunopositive for DHEA-ST (Fig. 3F and 3I), which may explain the incomplete suppression of the serum DHEA-S level.We did not evaluate the tumor using magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), which is a limitation in our case report. Changes in MRI signal intensity on chemical shift images are useful in the diagnosis of adrenal adenomas because they reflect lipid content [2]. However, in adenomas predominantly composed of compact cells, as in our patient, the benefit of MRI may be limited because of the poor lipid content. AVS is usually not required for the diagnosis of ACTH-independent Cushing syndrome in patients with a unilateral adrenal tumor. However, 131I-adosterol scintigraphy, which evaluates the laterality of cortisol overproduction, was negative in our patient. There have been a few cases reporting discordant results between CT findings and AVS lateralization, even in patients with a unilateral lesion [21]. Therefore, we performed AVS to confirm cortisol overproduction in the tumor.In conclusion, we encountered a case of ACTH-independent Cushing syndrome with atypical imaging findings. It is important to recognize in the differential diagnosis that cortisol-producing adrenal adenomas are rarely predominantly composed of compact cells, and such adenomas can present with atypical imaging findings, such as high unenhanced CT attenuation values, negative uptake on 131I-adosterol scintigraphy, and strong accumulation on 18F-FDG PET/CT.

Learning Points

Cortisol-producing adrenal adenomas are rarely predominantly composed of lipid-poor compact cells, displaying atypical radiological features.These adenomas can show high unenhanced CT attenuation values, reflecting poor lipid content.
131I-adosterol scintigraphy can show negative uptake in these adenomas, possibly because of increased cholesterol ester metabolism by HSL.A high SUVmax on 18F-FDG PET/CT can be observed, likely from enhanced glucose uptake via GLUT-1,3.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 27-year-old woman.
- **Chief Complaint:** None explicitly stated, but presented with features of Cushing syndrome.
- **History of Present Illness:** History of hypertension, dyslipidemia, weight gain, and a moon face. Diagnosed with hypertension and dyslipidemia at age 24 during a medical checkup and started treatment at 25.
- **Past Medical History:** None prior to 24 years of age.
- **Personal History:** None.
- **Family History:** Not specified.
- **Physical Examination:** Cushingoid features (e.g., moon face), blood pressure 144/90 mmHg despite antihypertensive treatment.
- **Ancillary Tests:**
  - **Laboratory Findings:** 
    - Low plasma ACTH (2.5 pg/mL). 
    - Elevated 24-hour urinary free cortisol (302 μg/day), nighttime serum cortisol, and incomplete cortisol suppression post-dexamethasone suppression test. 
    - Normal 24-hour urinary 17-ketosteroids, slightly decreased DHEA-S.
  - **Imaging:**
    - CT: Left adrenal tumor (2 cm) with high density (44 Hounsfield units) and heterogeneous enhancement.
    - 131I-adosterol scintigraphy: No uptake.
    - 18F-FDG PET/CT: High uptake with SUVmax of 14.9.
    - Adrenal venous sampling (AVS): Confirmed left adrenal tumor as the source of cortisol overproduction.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
- **Initial Considerations:** The dominant symptoms of Cushing syndrome and imaging findings pointed to possible ACTH-independent cortisol overproduction due to an adrenal pathology.
- **Step 1:** Cortisol-producing adenoma vs. adrenocortical carcinoma:
  - Adrenocortical carcinoma was considered because of the atypical imaging features (e.g., high CT attenuation, high SUVmax), but pathological findings (low Weiss score, Ki-67 index of 4%) ruled out malignancy.
- **Step 2:** Lipid-rich vs. lipid-poor adrenal adenoma:
  - Typical cortisol-producing adenomas exhibit lipid-rich characteristics with low CT attenuation, but histopathology revealed compact cells poor in lipid, explaining high CT attenuation and negative isotopic uptake on scintigraphy. 
- **Step 3:** Black adenoma:
  - Similar histology to black adenoma (lipofuscin-containing compact cells), but external appearance and scattered lipofuscin granules suggested it was a compact cell-dominant adrenal adenoma without black pigment.
- **Final Rationale:** Imaging findings were atypical due to the predominance of lipid-poor compact cells, explaining dense CT appearance, negative scintigraphy uptake, and high FDG-PET SUVmax. These findings, resolved by histopathological evidence, confirmed a rare presentation of a cortisol-producing adrenal adenoma.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Lipid-poor compact cell-dominant cortisol-producing adrenal adenoma resulting in ACTH-independent Cushing syndrome. Atypical imaging findings due to low lipid content and compact cell predominance.
generate_case.diagnosis_results: Lipid-poor compact cell-dominant cortisol-producing adrenal adenoma.

results.thinking_process:
Okay, let's tackle this case. So, the patient is a 27-year-old woman presenting with Cushingoid features: moon face, weight gain, hypertension, dyslipidemia. She was diagnosed with hypertension and dyslipidemia at 24, started treatment at 25. Now, her BP is still high despite meds. The key here is figuring out the cause of her Cushing syndrome.

First, the lab results show low ACTH. That points towards an ACTH-independent cause, right? Because in ACTH-dependent Cushing's (like pituitary or ectopic), ACTH would be normal or high. Low ACTH suggests the problem is in the adrenal glands themselves—adrenal Cushing's.

Next, the 24-hour urinary free cortisol is elevated. That confirms hypercortisolism. The dexamethasone suppression test was incomplete, which means cortisol didn't suppress properly. That's another confirmation of Cushing's. The normal 17-ketosteroids and slightly low DHEA-S might be clues. Since 17-KS are normal, it's less likely to be an adrenal carcinoma, which often secretes more androgens, leading to higher 17-KS. So maybe an adenoma instead?

Imaging: CT shows a left adrenal tumor, 2 cm, high density (44 HU), heterogeneous enhancement. Adrenal adenomas usually have lower HU (like <10), but this is higher. Wait, but some adenomas can have higher density. However, the scintigraphy with 131I-adosterol showed no uptake. Adosterol is taken up by adrenocortical cells. If there's no uptake, maybe the tumor isn't of adrenocortical origin? Wait, but adrenal adenomas usually take up adosterol. If there's no uptake, could it be a carcinoma? Or maybe a different type of tumor? But then the FDG-PET shows high uptake with SUVmax 14.9. Adrenal carcinomas are typically FDG-avid, whereas adenomas usually aren't. So high SUV here suggests malignancy. But the AVS confirmed the left adrenal as the source. Hmm. Also, the tumor is 2 cm—carcinomas are usually larger, but not always. 

Putting it together: Low ACTH, adrenal tumor, confirmed cortisol source via AVS. The conflicting imaging: CT suggests possible adenoma but high density. Scintigraphy negative, PET positive. Maybe this is an adrenal cortical carcinoma? Because carcinomas don't take up adosterol, have high FDG uptake. But the 17-KS are normal. Wait, but DHEA-S is slightly decreased. In adenomas, DHEA-S is often low because of cortisol suppression. Carcinomas may have mixed hormone secretion. 

Alternatively, could it be a cortisol-secreting adrenal adenoma that's not taking up adosterol? Maybe a rare case. Or perhaps the scintigraphy was done improperly. But AVS shows left adrenal is the source, so the tumor is definitely producing cortisol. The combination of low ACTH, adrenal mass, and high cortisol would confirm adrenal Cushing's. Now, distinguishing adenoma vs carcinoma. The size is 2 cm, which is more common for adenomas. Carcinomas are often >4 cm. But the high SUVmax on PET is concerning. Heterogeneous enhancement on CT can be seen in both, but higher HU might suggest something else. Also, adrenal carcinomas often have higher mitotic rate, necrosis, but imaging can't confirm that. 

Wait, in this case, the patient's age is 27. Adrenal carcinoma is more common in younger or older? Actually, it can occur at any age, but the median is around 40s-50s. Not sure. 

Another angle: The combination of high cortisol, low ACTH, and adrenal tumor. Most likely adrenal adenoma. But why the PET uptake? Maybe it's a metabolically active adenoma? Some adenomas can have FDG uptake, but typically lower than carcinomas. SUVmax of 14.9 is quite high. That's more in line with carcinoma. 

But then again, AVS confirmed the source. If it's carcinoma, maybe there's metastasis, but the case doesn't mention that. Maybe it's a rare case of a functional adrenal carcinoma presenting with Cushing's and high FDG uptake. But the 17-KS are normal. Hmm. 

Alternatively, could this be a cortisol-secreting adrenocortical adenoma with atypical imaging features? Maybe a lipid-poor adenoma which has higher HU on CT. Lipid-rich adenomas have lower HU. So if it's lipid-poor, the HU is higher. So maybe the CT is showing a lipid-poor adenoma. The scintigraphy not taking up adosterol—maybe the tumor is nonfunctional in terms of other steroids but just secretes cortisol. Wait, but the adosterol uptake is for adrenocortical tissue. If the tumor is adrenocortical, it should take up. But if it's a carcinoma, maybe it's dedifferentiated and doesn't take up. 

This is a bit confusing. The AVS confirmed the left adrenal as the source, so cortisol is coming from there. The combination of low ACTH, elevated cortisol, and imaging. The key is whether it's benign or malignant. The high FDG uptake is a red flag. But the size is small. Maybe the patient has an adrenocortical carcinoma. 

Alternatively, some adrenal adenomas can have higher FDG uptake. But generally, a SUVmax over 10 is more indicative of malignancy. So 14.9 is high. 

So, weighing all factors: The patient likely has an adrenal cause of Cushing's. The imaging suggests possible malignancy (high SUV), but the size is small. However, the final diagnosis would be adrenal Cushing's syndrome due to adrenocortical adenoma or carcinoma. But given the imaging and scintigraphy, perhaps carcinoma. 

Wait, but the 17-KS are normal. In carcinoma, there's often overproduction of multiple hormones, including androgens, leading to elevated 17-KS. But this patient's 17-KS are normal. That might lean towards adenoma. 

So maybe this is a cortisol-secreting adrenal adenoma that's lipid-poor (hence higher HU on CT), doesn't take up adosterol (maybe due to suppression or technical reasons), and has high FDG uptake for some reason. Alternatively, it's a carcinoma. 

The answer here probably is left adrenal cortisol-producing adenoma. But the FDG uptake is conflicting. Maybe the answer is adrenocortical carcinoma. Wait, but AVS confirmed the left adrenal as the source, and the CT shows a tumor. 

Alternatively, the combination of high cortisol, low ACTH, adrenal mass with AVS confirmation makes the diagnosis of adrenal Cushing's syndrome. The specific tumor type: considering the imaging, maybe carcinoma. But the age and normal 17-KS may suggest adenoma. 

Hmm. Let's check the features again. The tumor is 2 cm, 44 HU, heterogeneous. Adrenal adenomas usually <10 HU, but lipid-poor can be higher. 44 is quite high. Carcinomas can have higher HU. The scintigraphy no uptake: adrenocortical adenomas usually take up adosterol, so no uptake might be a problem. Maybe the tumor isn't taking it up because it's nonfunctional in other steroids, but is secreting cortisol. Or perhaps the scintigraphy wasn't properly done. 

The PET SUV is 14.9, which is high. Adrenal carcinomas usually have SUV >10. Adenomas usually <3. So this strongly suggests carcinoma. 

But the patient is 27, and the tumor is 2 cm. Carcinomas are usually larger, but exceptions exist. 

So maybe the final diagnosis is adrenocortical carcinoma. But wait, the answer might be adrenal adenoma. 

Alternatively, considering all the data: cortisol-secreting adrenal adenoma. Because the 17-KS are normal, DHEA-S slightly low (consistent with ACTH suppression), and even though the imaging is conflicting, AVS confirms the left adrenal. The high SUV could be a red herring. 

Wait, but FDG-PET is not specific. Some adenomas can have high uptake. Maybe in this case, the adenoma is FDG-avid. 

In the end, the key diagnostic points are the biochemical confirmation of ACTH-independent Cushing's (low ACTH, high cortisol, suppression test positive) and the imaging showing an adrenal tumor confirmed by AVS. The final diagnosis is adrenal Cushing's syndrome due to a cortisol-producing adrenal adenoma. However, the imaging findings (high HU, no adosterol uptake, high FDG uptake) might suggest carcinoma. But given the normal 17-KS and age, perhaps adenoma. 

But I'm a bit torn. The answer here is likely left adrenal adenoma, but the imaging suggests possible malignancy. Wait, the AVS confirmed left as the source. So the tumor is functional. The combination of low ACTH, high cortisol, and adrenal mass. The answer is adrenal Cushing's syndrome due to adrenal adenoma. But why the high FDG uptake? Maybe it's an atypical adenoma. 

Alternatively, given the high SUV and lack of adosterol uptake, the correct diagnosis is adrenocortical carcinoma. But 17-KS are normal. 

Hmm. Let me check: In adrenal adenomas, 131I-adosterol scintigraphy typically shows uptake, whereas in carcinomas, no uptake because they don't take up the tracer. So in this case, no uptake on scintigraphy, which would suggest carcinoma. Also, FDG-PET high SUV supports carcinoma. So even though the tumor is 2 cm, the imaging features suggest carcinoma. 

Therefore, the final diagnosis is adrenocortical carcinoma. But wait, the 17-KS are normal. In adrenal carcinoma, 17-KS are often elevated. So this is conflicting. 

Alternatively, maybe this is a case of primary pigmented nodular adrenal disease (PPNAD), but that's usually ACTH-independent, bilateral, and micronodules. Not applicable here. 

Another possibility: adrenal adenoma with myelolipomatous changes, but that's rare. 

Alternatively, metastatic lesion to the adrenal, but the patient has no history of cancer. 

So, considering all the evidence, the most probable diagnosis is cortisol-secreting adrenocortical carcinoma. The key points are the low ACTH, adrenal mass with imaging features suggesting malignancy (high HU, no adosterol uptake, high FDG avidity). The normal 17-KS might be a false reassurance, but not all carcinomas produce androgens. 

Therefore, the answer would be adrenocortical carcinoma. However, some sources say that most adrenal carcinomas are large (>4 cm) at diagnosis, but this one is 2 cm. So maybe a rare case. 

Alternatively, the answer might be adrenal adenoma. 

Wait, the AVS confirmed cortisol overproduction from the left adrenal. The CT shows a tumor there. So regardless of benign or malignant, the diagnosis is adrenal Cushing's. The specific tumor type is the question. 

The key here is differentiating adenoma vs carcinoma. The imaging suggests carcinoma. So despite the normal 17-KS, I'll lean towards carcinoma. 

Final answer: Left adrenocortical carcinoma.
results.content:
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient presents with clinical and biochemical features of Cushing syndrome, including hypertension, weight gain, moon face, elevated urinary free cortisol, and incomplete cortisol suppression on dexamethasone testing. Low plasma ACTH confirms ACTH-independent hypercortisolism, localizing the cause to the adrenal glands.  
<step 2> Imaging identifies a left adrenal tumor. The CT characteristics (2 cm size, 44 Hounsfield units, heterogeneous enhancement) raise suspicion for malignancy, as lipid-poor adenomas typically have lower density. The absence of 131I-adosterol uptake suggests non-adrenocortical origin or dedifferentiation, which is atypical for benign adenomas.  
<step 3> High FDG-PET SUVmax (14.9) strongly supports malignancy, as adrenocortical carcinomas (ACCs) exhibit marked metabolic activity compared to adenomas. However, the normal urinary 17-ketosteroids and only slightly decreased DHEA-S conflict with typical ACC features, which often include androgen excess.  
<step 4> Adrenal venous sampling (AVS) confirms the left adrenal tumor as the cortisol source. Despite the tumor’s small size (2 cm), the imaging discordance (high FDG uptake, no adosterol uptake, and high CT density) and biochemical evidence of isolated cortisol excess suggest a rare, early-stage adrenocortical carcinoma with atypical hormone secretion.  

### Answer:
Adrenocortical carcinoma (left adrenal tumor).

reasoning_eval.step: The patient presents with clinical and biochemical features of Cushing syndrome, including hypertension, weight gain, moon face, elevated urinary free cortisol, and incomplete cortisol suppression on dexamethasone testing. Low plasma ACTH confirms ACTH-independent hypercortisolism, localizing the cause to the adrenal glands.

Imaging identifies a left adrenal tumor. The CT characteristics (2 cm size, 44 Hounsfield units, heterogeneous enhancement) raise suspicion for malignancy, as lipid-poor adenomas typically have lower density. The absence of 131I-adosterol uptake suggests non-adrenocortical origin or dedifferentiation, which is atypical for benign adenomas.

High FDG-PET SUVmax (14.9) strongly supports malignancy, as adrenocortical carcinomas (ACCs) exhibit marked metabolic activity compared to adenomas. However, the normal urinary 17-ketosteroids and only slightly decreased DHEA-S conflict with typical ACC features, which often include androgen excess.

Adrenal venous sampling (AVS) confirms the left adrenal tumor as the cortisol source. Despite the tumor’s small size (2 cm), the imaging discordance (high FDG uptake, no adosterol uptake, and high CT density) and biochemical evidence of isolated cortisol excess suggest a rare, early-stage adrenocortical carcinoma with atypical hormone secretion.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: Initial considerations: Dominant symptoms and imaging findings suggested possible ACTH-independent cortisol overproduction due to an adrenal pathology.

Cortisol-producing adenoma vs. adrenocortical carcinoma: Adrenocortical carcinoma was considered due to atypical imaging features but was ruled out by pathological findings (low Weiss score, Ki-67 index of 4%).

Lipid-rich vs. lipid-poor adrenal adenoma: Typical cortisol-producing adenomas are lipid-rich with low CT attenuation; however, histopathology showed compact cells poor in lipid, explaining high CT attenuation and negative scintigraphy uptake.

Black adenoma: Histology similar to black adenoma (lipofuscin-containing compact cells), but external appearance and scattered lipofuscin granules indicated a compact cell-dominant adrenal adenoma without black pigment.

Final rationale: Imaging findings were atypical due to the predominance of lipid-poor compact cells, explaining the dense CT appearance, negative scintigraphy uptake, and high FDG-PET SUVmax. These findings, confirmed by histopathology, identified a rare presentation of a cortisol-producing adrenal adenoma.

Final diagnosis: Lipid-poor compact cell-dominant cortisol-producing adrenal adenoma resulting in ACTH-independent Cushing syndrome. Atypical imaging findings due to low lipid content and compact cell predominance.
